Case Study · Internship · 2023

Refreshing Fastrack's web experiencefor a younger audience

Brand Experience UI Design Youth Engagement Web + Mobile

As part of a design team at Unikwan during my internship, I worked on a refresh of Fastrack's website experience — rethinking content structure and visual language so the digital experience would feel as energetic and clear as the brand itself. The work covered both web and mobile.

🔒

This project was done during my internship at Unikwan, working with a team of designers on Fastrack's web and mobile redesign. Some details are adapted due to client confidentiality.

UI Designer · within design team

Overview

The project at a glance

Role
UI Designer (intern), within a design team
Client
Fastrack · Titan Group
Scope
Web + mobile redesign · brand-led UI

Specific sections · layout · visual exploration
Skills used
Brand Experience UI Design Visual Language Web + Mobile Team Collaboration Client Feedback
What got built

By the end of the internship, the redesigned web and mobile experience had been handed off — section structure rethought, visual language refreshed, and the overall feel pushed closer to Fastrack's brand energy. Whether the redesign was fully shipped or partially adopted is a question for the client; my contribution was the design work itself, done as part of a small team.

Problem

The experience wasn't carrying the brand's energy

Fastrack has a recognisable brand identity — youthful, bold, expressive. The existing website didn't quite reflect that. The brief identified four areas where the experience was falling short of the brand it was supposed to represent.

No clear narrative flow
Sections lacked a coherent story — the experience felt like a catalogue rather than a brand journey.
Inconsistent visual expression
Visual elements weren't cohesive enough to carry Fastrack's bold personality across the full experience.
Weak youth connection
The experience didn't feel current or expressive enough for Fastrack's core young audience — missing the energy the brand is known for.
Visuals vs usability imbalance
Bold visual intent without the structural backbone to support it — engaging in places but confusing to navigate.
Process

Structure first, expression second

The redesign needed to work across both structure and visuals — and crucially in that order. Understanding the brand and identifying the gaps came before any pixels were pushed.

01
Brand Audit
Tone · Audience · Identity
02
Evaluate
Existing gaps
03
Restructure
Section flow · Hierarchy
04
Explore
Visual directions
05
Iterate
Feedback · Refinement
Brand audit notes, content hierarchy sketches, and early layout thinking — most of the structural work happened on paper before Figma.
Team
Design Team at Unikwan Collaborative Iteration Client Feedback
Key Work

Three areas that shaped the redesign

The work moved through three connected focus areas — structure, expression, and the specific audience the site was actually for. Each shaped the next, and none of them worked in isolation.

Focus Area 01
Restructuring the experience
Hierarchy before aesthetics
Rethinking section order, content hierarchy, and how the brand narrative builds across the site.

A big part of the redesign was reorganising the site to flow better — rethinking section hierarchy, structuring content to guide users through a story instead of a catalogue, and tightening navigation. The goal was to reduce confusion without killing the energy that makes Fastrack feel alive.

The tension
Brand energy and structure tend to pull in opposite directions. Every structural decision had to serve the brand, not fight it.
Focus Area 02
Redefining the visual language
Bold without overwhelming
A more youthful and expressive UI direction — refreshed typography, layout hierarchy, and a more engaging use of imagery.

The UI was redesigned to reflect Fastrack's personality more accurately — a more youthful, expressive visual direction, improved typography and layout hierarchy, and a more deliberate use of imagery and components. The goal wasn't to make it louder; it was to make every loud moment intentional.

The balance
Keep the design bold without overwhelming users. Fastrack is loud, but the experience still needs to be easy to move through.
Mobile detail
A closer view of a refreshed mobile component.

Mobile got particular attention — younger audiences spend most of their time there, and a brand experience that holds together on desktop but collapses on a 5-inch screen isn't really holding together at all. Component-level decisions had to work in both directions.

Focus Area 03
Designing for youth engagement
Fast, visual, clear

The experience was shaped around how younger users tend to browse — fast, visual-first, with low tolerance for friction. The principles below were the working assumptions the visual and structural choices kept coming back to.

Fast
Decisions made in seconds — every screen had to land quickly.
Visual
Imagery drives engagement before copy gets read.
Clear
Clarity holds even when the visuals are running hot.
Before & after
Before
After
Side-by-side of one section — original on the left, refreshed direction on the right.
Ownership

Contributing within a design team

As an intern in a small design team at Unikwan, I owned specific deliverables while working closely with the rest of the team on overall direction. Not solo work — collaborative work with clear pockets of ownership.

🎨
Redesigning key website sections
Took ownership of specific sections — from layout decisions to final visual execution — within the team's direction.
📐
Content & layout decisions within sections
Structured content hierarchy at the section level — deciding what to show, in what order, and how the flow built.
🖌️
UI explorations aligned with the brand
Developed visual explorations that translated Fastrack's identity into specific UI patterns and components.
🤝
Iteration & collaboration
Worked closely with the rest of the design team to refine flows, align on direction, and rework based on feedback.
What Didn't Work

Iterations that grounded the direction

A few early directions didn't hold up. These weren't failures so much as the project narrowing toward a more honest answer.

Expression over structure, too early
Some early directions leaned into visual boldness before the structural foundation was solid — engaging in isolation, confusing in context.
Engaging but not usable
A few explorations felt exciting as visuals but fell apart as actual browsing experiences. Energy and usability have to coexist — one without the other isn't enough.
More iterations needed than expected
The clarity-vs-energy tension required more rounds than anyone planned for. Each cycle made the direction tighter though.
Learnings

How this shaped my thinking

This project shaped a lot of how I now think about the relationship between brand and experience. The lessons stuck even as my work moved into very different domains afterwards.

Before
Visuals and structure as separate concerns.
After
Strong visuals need strong structure — one without the other falls apart in real use.
Before
Designing for a generic user.
After
Designing for a specific audience changes every decision — type size, interaction speed, content tone.
Before
Storytelling as a writing problem.
After
Storytelling is a structural design problem — section order, pacing, and hierarchy all shape how a brand narrative lands.
Before
Iteration as fixing mistakes.
After
Iteration and collaboration are how real projects reach the right answer — not a sign of failure, but the actual process.
What's Next

Where this thinking goes next

Looking ahead
Brand, UI, and user behaviour — in higher-stakes contexts
This project sparked my interest in experiences where brand identity and user behaviour have to coexist — designing for how people feel as much as what they do. Beyond consumer brand work, I'm drawn to higher-stakes domains where design decisions carry more weight than visual energy alone.
Brand + UX
Consumer brand experiences
Products where brand and UX are genuinely integrated — not one decorating the other, but both shaping the experience together.
Healthcare
High-stakes UX
Environments where clarity and trust are non-negotiable — a natural step up from designing for discerning consumer audiences.
Systems
Design systems at scale
This project showed what breaks without a design system foundation — I want to be on the side that builds those foundations properly.
